title: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman recounts the November Southern Baptists of Texas Convention vote that rejected an abolitionist amendment to the heartbeat-bill resolution, then dissects the testimonies of Pastors Nathan Lino and Bart Barber as case studies in why incrementalist arguments collapse under scrutiny.
audiences: abolitionist activist preparing to argue at a denominational meeting
Southern Baptist pastor weighing how to respond to abolition resolutions
pro-life Christian skeptical of the abolitionist label
policy strategist comparing incremental vs. immediatist approaches

questions.question: What actually happened at the November Southern Baptists of Texas Convention regarding the abolition resolution?
questions.audience: abolitionist activist preparing to argue at a denominational meeting
questions.answer: The convention took up a Resolutions Committee proposal celebrating the Texas Heartbeat Act, and abolitionist Pastor Aaron Wright moved to strike its language and replace it with the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion. After testimony on both sides, the convention rejected the abolitionist amendment and adopted the heartbeat-bill resolution instead.
questions.quote: Pastor and abolitionist Aaron Wright motioned to amend the resolution by striking all of the language of the heartbeat bill resolution and replacing it with the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion.

questions.question: Why does Silberman think Nathan Lino's argument was especially weak?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing how to respond to abolition resolutions
questions.answer: Lino objected to calling abortion a crime against humanity that should be treated as murder by suggesting such language is harsh toward young women in church youth groups who might have sex outside of marriage. Silberman reads this as edging toward the position that warning Christian women away from killing their own children is itself unloving.
questions.quote: It seemed that he was on the verge of suggesting that it is unloving and mean-spirited to warn young, Christian women away from murdering their own children by calling abortion a crime against humanity that should be treated as murder.

questions.question: Does softening the language about abortion actually serve post-abortive mothers, as Lino implies?
questions.answer: Silberman argues the opposite: refusing to call abortion the killing of an equal human being denies post-abortive mothers access to the gospel of forgiveness, and it leaves possibly pre-abortive mothers thinking abortion is not that serious a sin.
questions.quote: To do so is unloving to post-abortive mothers because it denies them access to the gospel, and it’s unloving to possibly pre-abortive mothers because it makes them think abortion isn’t that bad.

questions.question: How much practical power does the church in states like Texas actually have to end abortion, in Silberman's view?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing how to respond to abolition resolutions
questions.answer: He claims the church in states like Texas and Oklahoma could end abortion almost immediately if Bible-believing churches repented of apathy, engaged the fight, and disciplined any legislator refusing to support the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act.
questions.quote: If every Bible-believing Church repented of apathy, got engaged in the battle, and announced that any legislator who refuses to do justice by supporting the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act would face Church discipline, abortion would be abolished.

questions.question: Why should I be wary of someone who leads with their pro-life credentials?
questions.audience: pro-life Christian skeptical of the abolitionist label
questions.answer: Silberman observes that politicians and pastors blocking abolition routinely open by reciting their pro-life resume. He argues the label has become so elastic that it functions as cover for opposing actual abolition, which is why he says listeners should listen for a concrete commitment to immediate abolition rather than to the brand.
questions.quote: Being super pro-life is how the pro-lifers at the top get away with keeping abortion legal. This is why it’s important to be suspicious of anyone calling themselves pro-life. Being pro-life can mean anything or nothing at all. What you need to listen for is a promise to fight for the immediate abolition of abortion.

questions.question: Did the abolitionist resolution really call SBTC leaders compromisers of the Word of God, as Lino claimed?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing how to respond to abolition resolutions
questions.answer: Silberman, who wrote the disputed sentence, says the resolution made a general claim that compromise is not the language of the bride of Christ rather than naming individuals, and that the underlying point that incremental laws compromise from the standard of "you shall not murder" is simply true.
questions.quote: Page five, sentence one, paragraph two (which I was privileged to write) reads: “Compromise is the language of worldly politicians, not of the bride of Christ.”

questions.question: Is it fair for Lino to call abolitionists harsh while Bart Barber says nearly identical things about abortion being a holocaust?
questions.audience: abolitionist activist preparing to argue at a denominational meeting
questions.answer: Silberman points out that Barber called abortion a holocaust and called for criminalizing all abortion in his own testimony, yet Lino would not accuse him of being mean. He sees the "abolitionists are mean" line as a deliberate, repeatedly used talking point of the pro-life establishment.
questions.quote: “aBoLiTiOnIsTs aRe mEaN” has been a favorite talking point of the Pro-Life Establishment for years. They believe it works, so they keep using it specifically against abolitionists.

questions.question: Does Bart Barber back up his claim to support total abolition with action on actual abolition bills?
questions.audience: policy strategist comparing incremental vs. immediatist approaches
questions.answer: Silberman searched for any record of Barber engaging the Texas abolition bills (HB3326, HB896, the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act) and found nothing relevant; the only "Bart Barber abolition" results were videos of him opposing abolitionism.
questions.quote: Search engine and social media searches for “Bart Barber HB3326,” “Bart Barber HB896,” and “Bart Barber Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act” all turn up no relevant results. Searches for “Bart Barber abolition” turns up only various videos of Barber opposing abolitionism.

questions.question: Is abolitionism the same as expecting abortion to end overnight?
questions.audience: policy strategist comparing incremental vs. immediatist approaches
questions.answer: No. Silberman argues abolitionists, like their 19th-century predecessors, expect a long struggle. The abolitionism vs. incrementalism dispute is not about how long the fight takes but about what the movement's demand should be.
questions.quote: The abolitionism-incrementalism debate does not revolve around how long the battle takes (though the abolitionist strategy will certainly achieve the desired end more quickly). The debate revolves around *what our demand should be*.

questions.question: On what authority does an abolitionist demand for immediate abolition rest, and why can't an incremental demand rest there too?
questions.audience: pro-life Christian skeptical of the abolitionist label
questions.answer: Silberman grounds the abolitionist demand in Scripture ("You shall not murder") and the Fourteenth Amendment's equal-protection clause. A 20-week ban or heartbeat bill, he argues, cannot stand on either, because by drawing an arbitrary line it implies that abortion before that line is permissible.
questions.quote: An incrementalist’s demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God’s Word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

questions.question: Why does Silberman claim incremental laws can actually move culture in the wrong direction?
questions.audience: policy strategist comparing incremental vs. immediatist approaches
questions.answer: Because compromise bills rest on compromised foundations, they cannot deliver a powerful prophetic witness, and in some cases they actively dehumanize the preborn children they leave unprotected by classifying them as non-persons under the law.
questions.quote: Compromise bills and arguments mean compromised foundations; compromised foundations mean no powerful prophetic witness from our demands; and no powerful prophetic witness from our demands means no positive cultural change from our demands.

questions.question: What's wrong with Barber's analogy comparing pro-life incrementalists to Schindler, Ten Boom, and Allied soldiers?
questions.audience: abolitionist activist preparing to argue at a denominational meeting
questions.answer: Silberman, citing J.R. Haas, says Barber conflates two different things: taking territory over time (which abolitionists endorse) versus passing unjust laws as a tactic. A good general fully liberates each town as he takes it; an incrementalist, in the analogy, frees only some hostages per town because doing it slowly is supposedly pragmatic.
questions.quote: Abolitionism is the strategy of a good general: completely freeing the hostages of the Nazis from one town to the next (just as abolitionists work to abolish abortion from State and country to the next). Incrementalism is like taking a town and only freeing some of the hostages because it’s “pragmatic” to do it slowly.

questions.question: How prevalent is abortion among churchgoing women, according to the article?
questions.answer: Silberman cites surveys finding that more than 40 percent of American mothers obtaining abortions are regular churchgoers, and notes abolitionists routinely see cars with church stickers and Jesus fish pulling into abortion mills.
questions.quote: Surveys have found that upwards of 40 percent of American mothers having abortions are regular churchgoers.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Silberman explicitly links to this article twice as the underlying case for why abolitionists reject incremental legislation.

related.title: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
related.reason: Provides the abolitionist read of the very Texas Heartbeat Act that the SBTC's resolution celebrated, the policy backdrop for this convention fight.

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution*, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: Reports the national SBC vote on the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion, the same resolution Aaron Wright tried to substitute at the SBTC.

related.title: SBC Pastors Publish "Resolution on Abolishing Abortion" for 2021 Convention Meeting
related.reason: Introduces the text of the resolution Wright moved to substitute, including the language Lino objected to as harsh.

related.title: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
related.reason: Direct follow-up profile of Bart Barber, one of the two testifiers Silberman dissects here.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Silberman links directly to this piece to support his argument about Jeff Leach and the pattern of pro-life awards going to politicians who block abolition.

related.title: Former ERLC Policy Director Matt Hawkins Glad SBC Squashed Abolition "Like a Bug"
related.reason: Provides earlier SBC-establishment context for the institutional resistance to abolition that Lino and Barber are continuing in this article.
